quiz.title: Geometry: Reflections and Symmetry
quiz.subtitle: Unit 12 Quiz 2: Reflecting Across Lines and Axes; Line Symmetry (10 Questions)
quiz.subject: Geometry

questions.question: A reflection is a transformation that:
options: Flips a figure over a line, creating a mirror image
Slides a figure in a straight line
Turns a figure about a fixed point
Enlarges or reduces a figure proportionally
questions.explanation: A reflection flips a figure over a line (the line of reflection). Each point and its image are the same perpendicular distance from the line, but on opposite sides. Reflections are rigid motions — they preserve distance and angle measure — but they reverse orientation.

questions.question: A point $P(4, -3)$ is reflected across the $x$-axis. What are the coordinates of its image $P'$?
questions.explanation: Reflection across the $x$-axis: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$. So $P(4, -3) \rightarrow P'(4, 3)$. The $x$-coordinate stays the same; the $y$-coordinate changes sign.

questions.question: A triangle has vertices $A(2, 1)$, $B(5, 4)$, and $C(3, -2)$. After reflecting across the $y$-axis, what are the coordinates of $B'$?
questions.explanation: Reflection across the $y$-axis: $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, y)$. So $B(5, 4) \rightarrow B'(-5, 4)$. The $y$-coordinate stays the same; the $x$-coordinate changes sign.

questions.question: A point $Q(-6, -2)$ is reflected across the line $y = x$. What are the coordinates of $Q'$?
questions.explanation: Reflection across $y = x$ swaps the coordinates: $(x, y) \rightarrow (y, x)$. So $Q(-6, -2) \rightarrow Q'(-2, -6)$. The $x$ and $y$ values trade places.

questions.question: A point $R(3, 5)$ is reflected across the line $y = -x$. What are the coordinates of $R'$?
questions.explanation: Reflection across $y = -x$: $(x, y) \rightarrow (-y, -x)$. So $R(3, 5) \rightarrow R'(-5, -3)$. Both coordinates swap and change sign.

questions.question: Point $S(2, 7)$ is reflected across the horizontal line $y = 4$. What are the coordinates of $S'$?
questions.explanation: The line $y = 4$ is horizontal. The point $S(2, 7)$ is $3$ units above $y = 4$. Its reflection must be $3$ units below: $4 - 3 = 1$. So $S'(2, 1)$. The $x$-coordinate does not change for horizontal line reflections.

questions.question: How many lines of symmetry does a regular pentagon have?
questions.explanation: A regular pentagon has $5$ lines of symmetry — one through each vertex and the midpoint of the opposite side. In general, a regular $n$-gon has $n$ lines of symmetry.

questions.question: Which of the following letters has exactly TWO lines of symmetry?
questions.explanation: The letter $H$ has two lines of symmetry: one vertical through the center and one horizontal through the middle. Letter $A$ has one vertical line. Letter $B$ has one horizontal line. Letter $E$ has one horizontal line.

questions.question: A line segment with endpoints $P(-1, 6)$ and $Q(3, 2)$ is reflected across the vertical line $x = 2$. What is the image of $P$?
questions.explanation: The line $x = 2$ is vertical. $P(-1, 6)$ is $3$ units left of $x = 2$. Its reflection is $3$ units right: $2 + 3 = 5$. So $P'(5, 6)$. The $y$-coordinate stays the same. $Q(3, 2)$ is $1$ unit right of $x = 2$, so $Q'(1, 2)$.

questions.question: A triangle with vertices $(1, 1)$, $(5, 1)$, and $(3, 5)$ is reflected across the $x$-axis and then across the $y$-axis. What single transformation gives the same result?
options: A rotation of $180^\circ$ about the origin
A reflection across the line $y = x$
A rotation of $90^\circ$ about the origin
questions.explanation: Reflection across $x$-axis: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$. Then across $y$-axis: $(x, -y) \rightarrow (-x, -y)$. The composition $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, -y)$ is equivalent to a $180^\circ$ rotation about the origin. Composition of two perpendicular line reflections equals a rotation of twice the angle between the lines.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You have mastered reflections across axes, lines $y = x$ and $y = -x$, horizontal and vertical lines, and line symmetry. You can find images, identify lines of symmetry, and compose reflections.
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Remember: (1) $x$-axis: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$, (2) $y$-axis: $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, y)$, (3) $y = x$: $(x, y) \rightarrow (y, x)$, (4) $y = -x$: $(x, y) \rightarrow (-y, -x)$, (5) For a line $y = k$, only the $y$-coordinate changes; for $x = h$, only the $x$-coordinate changes.
scoreMessages.50: A fair start. Focus on: (1) identifying the line of reflection and applying the correct coordinate rule, (2) for horizontal lines $y = k$, use $y' = 2k - y$, (3) for vertical lines $x = h$, use $x' = 2h - x$, (4) a regular $n$-gon has $n$ lines of symmetry, (5) reflections reverse orientation but preserve distance and angle.
scoreMessages.0: Keep practicing! Reflection rules: $x$-axis: $(x, y) \rightarrow (x, -y)$. $y$-axis: $(x, y) \rightarrow (-x, y)$. $y = x$: $(x, y) \rightarrow (y, x)$. $y = -x$: $(x, y) \rightarrow (-y, -x)$. Horizontal line $y = k$: the point is reflected so that the line is the perpendicular bisector. Line symmetry means the figure can be folded along a line and match itself exactly.
